terms.definition: A phrase describing the result when two or more numbers are combined together to make a total.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Three adds up to five when you put two more with it.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with counting on — children may count the first number again instead of starting from it.

terms.definition: Having a greater size, amount, or number when two things are compared.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which group is bigger — the one with three cubes or four cubes?
terms.common_errors: Confusing physical size with numerical value — a large numeral 2 is not bigger than a small numeral 5.

terms.definition: Small round marks used to represent quantities on dice, dominoes, or ten frames, supporting subitising and counting.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: How many dots can you see on the dice?

terms.definition: Two groups that are exactly the same size, or the result of adding a number to itself.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Double three is six because three and three makes six.
terms.common_errors: Confusing doubling with counting twice — saying double 3 is 3 instead of 6.

terms.definition: The number word for 80, representing eight groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Can you count in tens all the way to eighty?
terms.common_errors: Difficulty connecting the word to the numeral 80 or to eight tens.

terms.definition: The number word for 40, representing four groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Ten, twenty, thirty, forty — that is four groups of ten.
terms.common_errors: Spelling or saying "fourty" instead of forty.

terms.definition: Collections of objects put together, used to organise items for counting, sharing, or comparing.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put the animals into groups of two.
terms.common_errors: Not keeping groups equal when sharing — making uneven groups.

terms.definition: Recognising a quantity straight away without needing to count each item one by one.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Can you tell me how many dots there are instantly, without counting?

terms.definition: The amount that remains after sharing, grouping, or taking away from a set.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: We shared ten sweets between three children and there was one left over.
terms.common_errors: Ignoring remainders or trying to force equal groups by miscounting.

terms.definition: A phrase used to describe how a number can be composed or built from smaller parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Five is made of two and three.

terms.definition: A word used to describe the result of combining numbers together to form a total.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Two and three makes five.
terms.common_errors: Using makes interchangeably with equals without understanding the combining action.

terms.definition: A pair of numbers that combine to total ten, forming a key number bond.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Seven and three makes ten — they are a number bond to ten.
terms.common_errors: Only knowing some pairs (e.g. 5+5) and not others (e.g. 3+7).

terms.definition: A greater quantity or amount when comparing two groups or numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are more red bricks than blue bricks.
terms.common_errors: Confusing more with bigger — a spread-out group may look like more even with fewer items.

terms.definition: The number word for 90, representing nine groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What comes after ninety when we count in tens?
terms.common_errors: Difficulty connecting ninety to 90 and to nine tens.

terms.definition: Done rapidly, used in maths to describe fast recall or recognition of quantities and facts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Can you give me a quick answer — how many fingers am I holding up?

terms.definition: To identify and name a number, shape, or pattern by sight without working it out from scratch.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Can you recognise the number five when you see it on the number line?
terms.common_errors: Confusing similar-looking numerals such as 6 and 9.

terms.definition: To visually identify and notice mathematical features such as quantities, shapes, or patterns.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What do you see — how many butterflies are on the leaf?

terms.definition: The number word for 70, representing seven groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Fifty, sixty, seventy — what number comes next?
terms.common_errors: Confusing the -ty ending with the teens (seventeen vs seventy).

terms.definition: The number word for 60, representing six groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are sixty minutes in one hour.

terms.definition: To notice or identify a number, pattern, or mathematical feature, often used for quick visual recognition.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Can you spot which group has the most?

terms.definition: Equal in number, size, or amount when comparing two or more groups or objects.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Both towers have five bricks — they are the same.
terms.common_errors: Thinking objects must be identical (same colour, shape) to be the same amount.

terms.definition: The number word for 30, representing three groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Ten, twenty, thirty — we have counted three groups of ten.

terms.definition: Recognising a quantity by sight alone (subitising), rather than counting each item individually.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Can you tell me how many without counting? Just look and say the number.
terms.common_errors: Children may still silently count even when asked to subitise.
